subject: Secured Transactions
coverage_standard: NCBE-aligned Secured Transactions rule bank keyed to Article 9 of the Uniform Commercial Code. NCBE currently states that Article 9 (Secured Transactions) may be covered on the MEE through the February 2026 bar exam, and it will no longer be tested on the MEE effective with the July 2026 bar exam.

official_scope_reference.source_note: Areas of law that may be covered on the MEE include Article 9 (Secured Transactions) of the Uniform Commercial Code.
official_scope_reference.current_change_note: Effective with the July 2026 bar exam, Secured Transactions will no longer be tested on the MEE.

rules.title: Transactions Covered by Article 9
rules.prompt_question: What transactions are generally governed by Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: Article 9 generally governs any transaction, regardless of form, that creates a security interest in personal property or fixtures by contract, as well as agricultural liens, sales of accounts, chattel paper, payment intangibles, and promissory notes, and certain consignments.
rules.application_example: A lender taking a lien in a debtor's equipment by agreement falls within Article 9.
rules.common_trap: Article 9 applies based on substance, not labels used by the parties.

rules.title: Transactions Excluded from Article 9
rules.prompt_question: What transactions are generally excluded from Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: Article 9 generally does not apply to most landlord's liens, statutory liens other than agricultural liens, assignments of wages, transfers of interests in real property except fixtures and certain related collateral, tort claims other than commercial tort claims, and many transfers by operation of law.
rules.application_example: A mortgage on land is governed by real-property law, not Article 9, although a fixture filing may be relevant for attached goods.
rules.common_trap: Commercial tort claims can be original collateral under Article 9, but ordinary consumer tort claims generally are outside Article 9.

rules.title: Security Interest
rules.prompt_question: What is a security interest?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest is an interest in personal property or fixtures that secures payment or performance of an obligation.
rules.application_example: A bank's interest in a borrower's inventory to secure repayment of a loan is a security interest.
rules.common_trap: A true lease does not create a security interest, but a lease intended for security does.

rules.title: Classifying Collateral
rules.prompt_question: Why is collateral classification important under Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: Collateral must be classified because attachment, perfection, priority, and filing rules often depend on whether the collateral is goods, accounts, chattel paper, instruments, investment property, deposit accounts, documents, general intangibles, commercial tort claims, or other defined categories.
rules.application_example: Whether collateral is an instrument or a payment intangible determines how a secured party perfects its interest.
rules.common_trap: Classification depends on the debtor's primary use or the statutory definition, not the parties' labels.

rules.title: Goods
rules.prompt_question: What are goods under Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: Goods are all things that are movable when a security interest attaches, including fixtures, standing timber to be cut, and unborn young of animals, but not money, documents, instruments, accounts, chattel paper, or general intangibles.
rules.application_example: Farm equipment and inventory on store shelves are goods.
rules.common_trap: Goods are further subcategorized as consumer goods, inventory, farm products, or equipment based on the debtor's primary use.

rules.title: Consumer Goods
rules.prompt_question: What are consumer goods?
rules.rule_statement: Consumer goods are goods used or bought for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.
rules.application_example: A television purchased for home use is consumer goods.
rules.common_trap: The same item could be equipment or inventory if used in business instead of home life.

rules.title: Inventory
rules.prompt_question: What is inventory?
rules.rule_statement: Inventory is goods, other than farm products, that are leased by a person, held for sale or lease, furnished under a contract of service, consist of raw materials, work in process, or materials used or consumed in a business in a short period of time.
rules.application_example: A retailer's clothing stock is inventory.
rules.common_trap: Goods used over time in business are usually equipment, not inventory.

rules.title: Equipment
rules.prompt_question: What is equipment?
rules.rule_statement: Equipment is a residual category of goods consisting of goods used or bought for use primarily in business that are not inventory, farm products, or consumer goods.
rules.application_example: A restaurant's industrial oven is equipment.
rules.common_trap: If goods are held for sale, they are inventory rather than equipment.

rules.title: Farm Products
rules.prompt_question: What are farm products?
rules.rule_statement: Farm products are goods, other than standing timber, with respect to which the debtor is engaged in a farming operation and that are crops, livestock, supplies used or produced in farming, or products of crops or livestock in their unmanufactured states.
rules.application_example: A farmer's harvested corn is a farm product.
rules.common_trap: If sold to a merchant for resale, the goods may become inventory in the buyer's hands.

rules.title: Accounts
rules.prompt_question: What is an account under Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: An account is a right to payment for property sold, leased, licensed, assigned, or otherwise disposed of, or for services rendered, not evidenced by chattel paper or an instrument, and includes certain rights to payment arising from insurance, energy, credit cards, and other listed sources.
rules.application_example: A business's receivable from a customer for completed work is an account.
rules.common_trap: If the right to payment is evidenced by an instrument or chattel paper, it may fall into another category.

rules.title: Chattel Paper
rules.prompt_question: What is chattel paper?
rules.rule_statement: Chattel paper is a record or records that evidence both a monetary obligation and a security interest in specific goods, a security interest in specific goods and software used with the goods, a lease of specific goods, or a lease of specific goods and software used with the goods.
rules.application_example: An installment sale contract for a car is chattel paper.
rules.common_trap: Chattel paper is not the goods themselves; it is the record evidencing rights tied to the goods.

rules.title: Instrument
rules.prompt_question: What is an instrument under Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: An instrument is a negotiable instrument or another writing that evidences a right to payment of a monetary obligation, is not itself a security agreement or lease, and is of a type that in ordinary course is transferred by delivery with any necessary endorsement or assignment.
rules.application_example: A promissory note payable to order is an instrument.
rules.common_trap: An electronic record generally is not an instrument unless the statute specifically treats it as such.

rules.title: General Intangibles
rules.prompt_question: What are general intangibles?
rules.rule_statement: General intangibles are personal property, including things in action, other than accounts, chattel paper, deposit accounts, documents, goods, instruments, investment property, letter-of-credit rights, money, and oil, gas, or other minerals before extraction.
rules.application_example: A software license or patent right is usually a general intangible.
rules.common_trap: Payment intangibles are a special subset of general intangibles.

rules.title: Payment Intangible
rules.prompt_question: What is a payment intangible?
rules.rule_statement: A payment intangible is a general intangible under which the account debtor's principal obligation is a monetary obligation.
rules.application_example: A right to receive payment under a loan participation agreement may be a payment intangible.
rules.common_trap: Payment intangibles are automatically perfected upon sale in many cases, but not when merely used as collateral for a loan.

rules.title: Deposit Account
rules.prompt_question: What is a deposit account?
rules.rule_statement: A deposit account is a demand, time, savings, passbook, or similar account maintained with a bank, but it does not include investment property or accounts evidenced by an instrument.
rules.application_example: A business checking account is a deposit account.
rules.common_trap: A security interest in a deposit account as original collateral is perfected only by control, not by filing.

rules.title: Investment Property
rules.prompt_question: What is investment property?
rules.rule_statement: Investment property includes certificated securities, uncertificated securities, security entitlements, securities accounts, commodity contracts, and commodity accounts.
rules.application_example: A debtor's brokerage account holding stocks is investment property.
rules.common_trap: Perfection can be achieved by filing, but control often gives superior priority.

rules.title: Documents
rules.prompt_question: What are documents under Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: Documents are documents of title, including bills of lading, dock warrants, dock receipts, warehouse receipts, and orders for delivery of goods, and may be tangible or electronic if recognized by law.
rules.application_example: A warehouse receipt for stored grain is a document.
rules.common_trap: The document represents rights in the goods and may be negotiated separately from the goods.

rules.title: Commercial Tort Claim
rules.prompt_question: What is a commercial tort claim?
rules.rule_statement: A commercial tort claim is a claim arising in tort with respect to which the claimant is an organization or, if the claimant is an individual, the claim arose in the course of the claimant's business or profession and does not include damages for personal injury or death.
rules.application_example: A corporation's claim for business disparagement may be a commercial tort claim.
rules.common_trap: Commercial tort claims must be described specifically in the security agreement and after-acquired clauses generally do not reach them automatically.

rules.title: Proceeds
rules.prompt_question: What are proceeds under Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: Proceeds include whatever is acquired upon the sale, lease, license, exchange, or other disposition of collateral; whatever is collected on or distributed on account of collateral; rights arising out of collateral; claims arising out of loss or damage to collateral; and insurance payable by reason of loss or damage to the collateral.
rules.application_example: Cash received from selling inventory collateral is proceeds.
rules.common_trap: A security interest usually attaches automatically to identifiable proceeds.

rules.title: After-Acquired Property Clause
rules.prompt_question: When does an after-acquired property clause create a security interest in property acquired later?
rules.rule_statement: A security agreement may provide for a security interest in after-acquired collateral, except that an after-acquired property clause generally does not attach to consumer goods acquired more than 10 days after the secured party gives value or to commercial tort claims.
rules.application_example: A lender taking a lien in all present and future inventory reaches later-purchased inventory.
rules.common_trap: The consumer-goods limitation is narrow and often tested.

rules.title: Attachment Requirements
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a security interest to attach?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest attaches and becomes enforceable against the debtor and third parties when value has been given, the debtor has rights in the collateral or the power to transfer rights in the collateral, and the debtor has authenticated a security agreement that reasonably describes the collateral, or the secured party has possession or control of the collateral pursuant to the debtor's security agreement.
rules.application_example: A signed agreement granting a lender a security interest in the debtor's equipment, coupled with a loan advance, causes attachment once the debtor owns the equipment.
rules.common_trap: Perfection is separate from attachment; a security interest cannot be perfected before it attaches, though filing may occur before attachment and become effective upon attachment.

rules.title: Value Given
rules.prompt_question: What counts as value for attachment?
rules.rule_statement: Value includes a binding commitment to extend credit, the extension of credit, acceptance of delivery under a preexisting contract, and generally any consideration sufficient to support a simple contract.
rules.application_example: A bank's promise to make a loan is value even before all loan proceeds are disbursed.
rules.common_trap: Past consideration without a present commitment may raise attachment questions in some fact patterns.

rules.title: Debtor's Rights in Collateral
rules.prompt_question: What rights in collateral must the debtor have for attachment?
rules.rule_statement: The debtor must have rights in the collateral, or the power to transfer rights in the collateral to a secured party, before a security interest can attach.
rules.application_example: A buyer who has obtained voidable title may still have sufficient rights for attachment.
rules.common_trap: A mere expectancy generally is not enough, though future rights may allow later automatic attachment if the security agreement so provides.

rules.title: Security Agreement Requirements
rules.prompt_question: What must a security agreement contain?
rules.rule_statement: Unless possession or control substitutes for a signed record, a security agreement must be authenticated by the debtor and must reasonably identify the collateral.
rules.application_example: A signed agreement granting a security interest in 'all equipment' is generally sufficient.
rules.common_trap: The financing statement's broad language and the security agreement's sufficiency are distinct issues.

rules.title: Reasonable Description of Collateral
rules.prompt_question: How may collateral be reasonably described in a security agreement?
rules.rule_statement: A description of collateral is sufficient if it reasonably identifies what is described, including by specific listing, category, type of collateral defined in the UCC, quantity, computational formula, or any other method that objectively determines the collateral. But a supergeneric description such as 'all the debtor's assets' or 'all personal property' is generally insufficient in a security agreement.
rules.application_example: A description of 'all inventory and equipment' is ordinarily valid.
rules.common_trap: Supergeneric descriptions are usually allowed in financing statements, not in security agreements.

rules.title: Attachment by Possession
rules.prompt_question: When may possession substitute for a signed security agreement?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest may be enforceable without an authenticated security agreement if the collateral is in the possession of the secured party pursuant to the debtor's security agreement.
rules.application_example: A pawnbroker's possession of pledged jewelry can satisfy the enforceability requirement.
rules.common_trap: Possession works only for collateral that can be possessed and must be pursuant to agreement, not accidental custody.

rules.title: Attachment by Control
rules.prompt_question: When may control substitute for a signed security agreement?
rules.rule_statement: For collateral such as deposit accounts, electronic chattel paper, investment property, or letter-of-credit rights, a security interest may be enforceable if the secured party has control pursuant to the debtor's security agreement.
rules.application_example: A bank with control over a debtor's deposit account may have an enforceable security interest without a separate signed collateral record if the control arrangement is pursuant to agreement.
rules.common_trap: Control is both an attachment/perfection method for some collateral and a priority booster for certain disputes.

rules.title: General Methods of Perfection
rules.prompt_question: How may a security interest generally be perfected?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest is generally perfected by filing a financing statement, taking possession of the collateral, obtaining control of the collateral, or, in some cases, automatically upon attachment.
rules.application_example: A lender can perfect a lien in equipment by filing a financing statement.
rules.common_trap: The proper method depends on the collateral type; filing does not work for every type of original collateral.

rules.title: Financing Statement Requirements
rules.prompt_question: What must a financing statement contain?
rules.rule_statement: A financing statement is sufficient if it provides the debtor's name, the name of the secured party or its representative, and indicates the collateral covered.
rules.application_example: A properly completed UCC-1 naming the debtor and secured party and listing collateral is sufficient to file.
rules.common_trap: The financing statement does not itself create the security interest; attachment still requires a security agreement or substitute.

rules.title: Collateral Description in Financing Statement
rules.prompt_question: How broad may a collateral description be in a financing statement?
rules.rule_statement: A financing statement sufficiently indicates collateral if it describes the collateral pursuant to UCC methods or if it states that it covers all assets or all personal property of the debtor.
rules.application_example: A UCC-1 stating 'all assets' is generally sufficient.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse this with the stricter collateral-description rule for the security agreement.

rules.title: Debtor Name for Registered Organization
rules.prompt_question: How must a registered organization debtor be named on a financing statement?
rules.rule_statement: For a registered organization, the financing statement must provide the name stated on the public organic record most recently filed with or issued by the jurisdiction of organization.
rules.application_example: A corporation's exact filed corporate name must appear on the UCC-1.
rules.common_trap: Minor deviations in the debtor name can make the filing seriously misleading and ineffective.

rules.title: Debtor Name for Individual
rules.prompt_question: How should an individual debtor's name be listed on a financing statement?
rules.rule_statement: For an individual debtor, the financing statement must comply with the applicable jurisdiction's rule, often requiring either the name on the debtor's unexpired driver's license or, in some jurisdictions, the individual's correct personal name.
rules.application_example: If the state follows the driver's-license rule, the filing should use the name exactly as shown on the debtor's license.
rules.common_trap: Using nicknames or trade names is risky and may render the filing ineffective.

rules.title: Trade Name
rules.prompt_question: Is a trade name alone sufficient for the debtor's name on a financing statement?
rules.rule_statement: A financing statement that provides only the debtor's trade name does not sufficiently provide the debtor's name.
rules.application_example: Filing only under 'Bob's Bikes' is ineffective if the debtor is Robert Smith.
rules.common_trap: A trade name may be added, but it cannot substitute for the debtor's proper legal name.

rules.title: Seriously Misleading Errors
rules.prompt_question: When is an error in a financing statement seriously misleading?
rules.rule_statement: A financing statement that fails sufficiently to provide the debtor's name is seriously misleading unless a search of the filing office records under the debtor's correct name, using the filing office's standard search logic, would disclose the record.
rules.application_example: A misspelled debtor name may still be effective if a standard search under the correct name reveals the filing.
rules.common_trap: This safe harbor usually applies only to debtor-name errors, not all possible filing defects.

rules.title: Where to File
rules.prompt_question: Where is a financing statement generally filed?
rules.rule_statement: Except for certain fixture filings, timber to be cut, and as-extracted collateral, a financing statement is generally filed in the central filing office of the debtor's location.
rules.application_example: For most collateral, filing is made with the secretary of state or similar central office in the debtor's location.
rules.common_trap: Real-estate-related collateral like fixtures often requires local real-estate filing.

rules.subtopic: Choice of Law and Filing Location
rules.title: Location of Individual Debtor
rules.prompt_question: Where is an individual debtor located for Article 9 purposes?
rules.rule_statement: An individual debtor is located at the individual's principal residence.
rules.application_example: A consumer living primarily in Illinois is located in Illinois for filing purposes.
rules.common_trap: Temporary presence in another state usually does not change the debtor's Article 9 location.

rules.subtopic: Choice of Law and Filing Location
rules.title: Location of Registered Organization
rules.prompt_question: Where is a registered organization located for Article 9 purposes?
rules.rule_statement: A registered organization organized under state law is located in its state of organization.
rules.application_example: A Delaware corporation is located in Delaware for Article 9 filing purposes.
rules.common_trap: The location is not where its headquarters or principal place of business sits.

rules.subtopic: Choice of Law and Filing Location
rules.title: Location of Unregistered Organization
rules.prompt_question: Where is an unregistered organization located for Article 9 purposes?
rules.rule_statement: An organization that is not a registered organization is generally located at its place of business if it has only one, or at its chief executive office if it has more than one place of business.
rules.application_example: A general partnership operating in several states is usually located at its chief executive office.
rules.common_trap: This location rule matters for both filing office and governing law on perfection.

rules.title: Duration of Financing Statement
rules.prompt_question: How long is a financing statement effective?
rules.rule_statement: A filed financing statement is generally effective for five years from the date of filing unless a continuation statement is timely filed.
rules.application_example: A lender that filed on January 1, 2025, generally remains perfected until January 1, 2030.
rules.common_trap: If no continuation statement is timely filed, perfection lapses and may become retroactively vulnerable to intervening buyers or lien creditors.

rules.title: Continuation Statement
rules.prompt_question: When must a continuation statement be filed?
rules.rule_statement: A continuation statement may be filed only within six months before the financing statement would lapse, and timely filing continues effectiveness for another five years.
rules.application_example: A continuation filed nine months before lapse is ineffective.
rules.common_trap: The six-month window is strictly tested.

rules.title: Effect of Lapse
rules.prompt_question: What happens when a financing statement lapses?
rules.rule_statement: Upon lapse, the financing statement ceases to be effective and the security interest becomes unperfected, and it is deemed never to have been perfected as against a purchaser of the collateral for value.
keywords: deemed never perfected against purchaser for value
rules.application_example: A later lender that searches after lapse may take priority over the original lender.
rules.common_trap: The retroactive loss can be severe in priority disputes.

rules.title: Perfection by Possession
rules.prompt_question: For what collateral may a security interest be perfected by possession?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest in tangible negotiable documents, goods, instruments, money, or tangible chattel paper may generally be perfected by the secured party's possession of the collateral.
rules.application_example: A lender perfects a lien in a promissory note by taking possession of the original note.
rules.common_trap: Possession of money is usually the only way to perfect a security interest in money.

rules.title: Perfection of Deposit Accounts by Control
rules.prompt_question: How is a security interest in a deposit account perfected?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest in a deposit account as original collateral may be perfected only by control.
rules.application_example: A bank perfects its interest in a customer's account by being the bank with which the account is maintained.
rules.common_trap: Filing a financing statement covering the deposit account is ineffective as a perfection method for original collateral.

rules.title: Control of Deposit Account
rules.prompt_question: How does a secured party obtain control of a deposit account?
rules.rule_statement: A secured party has control of a deposit account if the secured party is the bank with which the account is maintained, the debtor, secured party, and bank agree in an authenticated record that the bank will comply with the secured party's instructions regarding the account without further consent by the debtor, or the secured party becomes the bank's customer with respect to the account.
keywords: bank with which account is maintained
rules.application_example: A lender enters a tri-party control agreement with the debtor and depositary bank.
rules.common_trap: A mere promise by the debtor to follow the lender's instructions is not enough; the bank must agree or the lender must be the bank itself or customer on the account.

rules.title: Control of Investment Property
rules.prompt_question: Why is control important for investment property?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest in investment property may be perfected by filing or control, but a secured party with control generally enjoys priority over a secured party who perfects only by filing.
rules.application_example: A lender takes control of a securities account through a control agreement and defeats an earlier filer without control.
rules.common_trap: Control often matters more than timing in investment-property priority disputes.

rules.title: Automatic Perfection of PMSI in Consumer Goods
rules.prompt_question: When is a PMSI in consumer goods automatically perfected?
rules.rule_statement: A purchase-money security interest in consumer goods is automatically perfected upon attachment, except for goods subject to certificate-of-title statutes and certain other exceptions.
rules.application_example: A store that finances a refrigerator purchased for home use generally has an automatically perfected PMSI.
rules.common_trap: Cars covered by certificate-of-title law usually require compliance with title notation rules, not reliance on automatic perfection.

rules.title: Automatic Perfection in Sale of Payment Intangible or Promissory Note
rules.prompt_question: When is perfection automatic upon sale of certain payment rights?
rules.rule_statement: A sale of a payment intangible or promissory note is automatically perfected upon attachment.
rules.application_example: A company that sells a promissory note outright may have automatic perfection in the sold note.
rules.common_trap: This automatic perfection does not apply merely because the note is pledged as collateral for a loan rather than sold.

rules.title: Certificate-of-Title Goods
rules.prompt_question: How is a security interest in goods covered by a certificate of title generally perfected?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest in goods covered by a certificate-of-title statute is generally perfected by compliance with that statute, usually by notation of the lien on the certificate of title, rather than by filing a financing statement.
rules.application_example: A lender financing a car purchase usually perfects by having its lien noted on the title.
rules.common_trap: Automatic perfection of a PMSI in consumer goods usually does not apply to certificate-of-title vehicles.

rules.title: Fixture Filing
rules.prompt_question: What is a fixture filing?
rules.rule_statement: A fixture filing is a financing statement covering goods that are or are to become fixtures and that is filed in the office where a mortgage on the related real property would be recorded and indicates that it covers fixtures, indicates the real property, and, if the debtor does not have an interest of record in the real property, provides the name of the record owner.
rules.application_example: A lender financing installed industrial equipment may perfect with a fixture filing in local land records.
rules.common_trap: A central filing may perfect against personal-property claimants, but fixture priority against real-estate interests often requires a true fixture filing.

rules.title: Temporary Perfection in Proceeds
rules.prompt_question: How long is a security interest automatically perfected in proceeds?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected security interest in proceeds is automatically perfected for 20 days after the security interest attaches to the proceeds. Continued perfection beyond 20 days generally requires satisfaction of one of the statutory continuation rules, such as filing covering the proceeds, same-office filing sufficiency, or identifiable cash proceeds.
rules.application_example: A perfected inventory lender is automatically perfected in cash proceeds from inventory sales for at least 20 days.
rules.common_trap: Cash proceeds often remain perfected beyond 20 days if identifiable.

rules.title: Identifiable Proceeds
rules.prompt_question: What are identifiable proceeds?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest continues in identifiable proceeds, and proceeds are identifiable if they can be traced back to the original collateral, including by equitable tracing methods such as the lowest intermediate balance rule for commingled cash proceeds.
rules.application_example: Cash proceeds deposited into a bank account may still be identifiable under tracing rules.
rules.common_trap: Once proceeds are no longer traceable, the security interest in those proceeds is lost.

rules.title: General Priority Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the general priority rule among conflicting perfected security interests?
rules.rule_statement: As between conflicting perfected security interests in the same collateral, priority generally dates from the earlier of filing or perfection, provided there is no period thereafter when there is neither filing nor perfection.
rules.application_example: Lender A files first and perfects later; Lender B perfects later without earlier filing; A has priority.
rules.common_trap: The race is to file or perfect, whichever occurs first, not merely to attach.

rules.title: Perfected Versus Unperfected
rules.prompt_question: Who wins between a perfected and an unperfected security interest?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected security interest generally has priority over a conflicting unperfected security interest.
rules.application_example: A lender with a filed financing statement defeats another lender whose interest attached but was never perfected.
rules.common_trap: Some buyers and lien creditors can still beat a perfected secured party under specific statutory rules.

rules.title: Unperfected Versus Unperfected
rules.prompt_question: Who wins between conflicting unperfected security interests?
rules.rule_statement: As between conflicting unperfected security interests, priority generally goes to the first to attach or become enforceable.
rules.application_example: If both lenders failed to perfect, the earlier attached lien generally wins.
rules.common_trap: Because neither interest is perfected, both remain vulnerable to lien creditors and many buyers.

rules.title: Lien Creditor Versus Secured Party
rules.prompt_question: Who wins between a lien creditor and a secured party?
rules.rule_statement: A lien creditor generally takes priority over an unperfected security interest but is subordinate to a perfected security interest. A lien creditor also loses to a security interest that becomes perfected by filing before the lien arises, even if attachment occurs later.
rules.application_example: A judgment creditor who levies before the lender perfects usually beats that lender.
rules.common_trap: Bankruptcy trustees often have the status of a hypothetical lien creditor.

rules.title: PMSI in Inventory
rules.prompt_question: How does a PMSI in inventory obtain superpriority?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected purchase-money security interest in inventory has priority over a conflicting security interest in the same inventory and its identifiable cash proceeds if the PMSI is perfected when the debtor receives possession of the inventory and the PMSI secured party sends an authenticated notice to the holder of the conflicting security interest before the debtor receives possession, stating that the PMSI secured party expects to acquire a PMSI in the debtor's inventory.
rules.application_example: A supplier financing incoming inventory sends proper notice to the prior blanket inventory lender before delivery and files before delivery.
rules.common_trap: The notice and timing requirements are strict and often tested.

rules.title: PMSI in Equipment
rules.prompt_question: How does a PMSI in equipment obtain superpriority?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected purchase-money security interest in equipment has priority over a conflicting security interest in the same equipment if the PMSI is perfected when the debtor receives possession of the equipment or within 20 days thereafter.
rules.application_example: A lender financing machinery files within 20 days after the debtor receives it and gains PMSI superpriority over an earlier blanket equipment filer.
rules.common_trap: Unlike inventory PMSIs, no advance notice to earlier secured parties is generally required.

rules.title: PMSI in Consumer Goods
rules.prompt_question: What priority benefit does a PMSI in consumer goods receive?
rules.rule_statement: A purchase-money security interest in consumer goods may receive PMSI priority and is often automatically perfected upon attachment, though certificate-of-title rules and buyer rules can affect the outcome.
rules.application_example: A store financing furniture bought for personal use often has PMSI priority over earlier non-PMSI claims.
rules.common_trap: Priority disputes involving consumer goods can also invoke garage-sale and buyer rules.

rules.title: Purchase-Money Security Interest Defined
rules.prompt_question: What is a purchase-money security interest?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest is purchase-money to the extent that the goods are purchase-money collateral with respect to that security interest, meaning the obligation is incurred as all or part of the price of the collateral or for value given to enable the debtor to acquire rights in or use of the collateral, if the value is in fact so used.
rules.application_example: A seller who finances the purchase price of equipment has a PMSI in that equipment.
rules.common_trap: A refinancing or cross-collateralized obligation can complicate PMSI status, especially in consumer-goods transactions.

rules.title: Buyer in Ordinary Course of Business
rules.prompt_question: When does a buyer in ordinary course take free of a security interest?
rules.rule_statement: A buyer in ordinary course of business, other than one buying farm products from a person engaged in farming operations, takes free of a security interest created by the buyer's seller even if the security interest is perfected and the buyer knows of its existence, so long as the buyer buys in good faith, without knowledge that the sale violates another's rights, and in ordinary course from a seller in the business of selling goods of that kind.
rules.application_example: A customer buying a television from a retail electronics store takes free of the store lender's inventory lien.
rules.common_trap: The security interest must have been created by the buyer's seller; upstream interests can raise different issues.

rules.title: Consumer-to-Consumer Buyer
rules.prompt_question: When does a buyer of consumer goods take free of a security interest?
rules.rule_statement: A buyer of consumer goods from a consumer seller takes free of a security interest, even if perfected, if the buyer buys without knowledge of the security interest, for value, primarily for the buyer's personal, family, or household purposes, and before a financing statement covering the goods has been filed.
rules.application_example: A person buys a used couch from a neighbor at a garage sale without knowledge of the neighbor's purchase-money lender's lien and takes free if no financing statement was filed.
rules.common_trap: If a financing statement was filed, this special buyer protection does not apply.

rules.title: Other Buyers of Goods
rules.prompt_question: What rights does a buyer not in ordinary course take in goods?
rules.rule_statement: A buyer of goods who is not a buyer in ordinary course generally takes subject to a perfected security interest but may take free of an unperfected security interest if the buyer gives value and receives delivery without knowledge of the security interest.
keywords: takes subject to perfected security interest
rules.application_example: A purchaser buying business equipment in a private sale usually takes subject to any perfected equipment lien.
rules.common_trap: Delivery matters in the rule protecting a buyer from an unperfected interest.

rules.title: Priority in Chattel Paper
rules.prompt_question: How can a purchaser of chattel paper get priority?
rules.rule_statement: A purchaser of chattel paper who gives new value and takes possession of tangible chattel paper or obtains control of electronic chattel paper in good faith, in the ordinary course of the purchaser's business, and without knowledge that the purchase violates another's rights may obtain priority over a security interest in the chattel paper claimed merely as proceeds, and in some circumstances over competing chattel-paper interests if the records indicate the purchaser's rights.
rules.application_example: A finance company buying retail installment contracts may defeat a prior inventory lender claiming the contracts as proceeds.
rules.common_trap: The special priority rule is technical and often depends on possession or control plus record-indication rules.

rules.title: Priority in Instruments
rules.prompt_question: When does possession of an instrument matter for priority?
rules.rule_statement: A purchaser of an instrument who gives value and takes possession in good faith and without knowledge that the purchase violates another's rights may have priority over a perfected security interest perfected only by filing.
rules.application_example: A bank that takes possession of a negotiable note may defeat an earlier filer that never took possession.
rules.common_trap: For instruments, possession is especially powerful in priority disputes.

rules.title: Priority in Deposit Accounts
rules.prompt_question: How is priority determined in deposit accounts?
rules.rule_statement: As among conflicting security interests in the same deposit account perfected by control, priority generally goes first to the secured party having control by becoming the bank's customer, then to the depositary bank itself, and then to secured parties with control agreements, with time of obtaining control often relevant among equal classes.
rules.application_example: A lender added as the bank's customer on the account may defeat another lender relying only on a control agreement.
rules.common_trap: Filing is irrelevant because original deposit-account collateral cannot be perfected by filing.

rules.title: Priority in Investment Property
rules.prompt_question: What priority advantage comes with control of investment property?
rules.rule_statement: A secured party who has control of investment property generally has priority over a secured party who does not have control.
rules.application_example: A lender with control of a securities account defeats an earlier filer without control.
rules.common_trap: For investment property, control often trumps earlier filing.

rules.title: Priority in Proceeds
rules.prompt_question: How is priority determined in proceeds?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected security interest in proceeds generally has the same date of priority as the perfected security interest in the original collateral so long as perfection in the proceeds continues.
keywords: same date of priority as original collateral
rules.application_example: An inventory lender's priority in inventory generally carries into identifiable cash proceeds from inventory sales.
rules.common_trap: If perfection in proceeds lapses, the carryover priority may be lost.

rules.title: Future Advances
rules.prompt_question: How do future advances affect priority?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected security interest secures future advances if the security agreement so provides, and the priority of those future advances generally relates back to the original filing or perfection against competing secured parties and lien creditors, subject to special rules in some contexts.
rules.application_example: A bank with a perfected blanket lien may continue making advances and retain its original priority date.
rules.common_trap: Some exam questions test whether the advance was authorized by the agreement and whether a special exception applies.

rules.title: Default
rules.prompt_question: What counts as default under Article 9?
rules.rule_statement: Article 9 does not define default, so default is determined by the parties' agreement and applicable law.
rules.application_example: Missing a payment or violating a debt-service covenant may constitute default if the contract says so.
rules.common_trap: Because Article 9 leaves default largely to contract, exam questions often assume default has occurred and focus on remedies.

rules.title: Self-Help Repossession
rules.prompt_question: When may a secured party repossess collateral without judicial process?
rules.rule_statement: After default, a secured party may take possession of collateral without judicial process if it can be done without breach of the peace.
rules.application_example: A repo company may quietly seize a car from a public lot if no confrontation or disturbance occurs.
rules.common_trap: A breach of the peace can expose the secured party to damages and may invalidate the repossession method.

rules.title: Breach of the Peace
rules.prompt_question: What is a breach of the peace in repossession?
rules.rule_statement: A breach of the peace includes conduct likely to lead to violence, force, or public disturbance, such as breaking into locked premises or continuing repossession over the debtor's contemporaneous objection.
rules.application_example: A secured party that enters a locked garage without consent likely breaches the peace.
rules.common_trap: Even police involvement can create problems if officers help the secured party repossess rather than merely keep order.

rules.title: Collection of Accounts and Payment Rights
rules.prompt_question: What may a secured party do with accounts after default?
rules.rule_statement: After default, a secured party may notify account debtors or other persons obligated on collateral to make payment directly to the secured party and may enforce the debtor's rights to payment.
rules.application_example: A lender with a lien on receivables tells customers to send payments directly to the lender.
rules.common_trap: In some cases a secured party may collect even before default if the agreement so provides.

rules.title: Disposition of Collateral
rules.prompt_question: How may a secured party dispose of collateral after default?
rules.rule_statement: After default, a secured party may sell, lease, license, or otherwise dispose of collateral in its present condition or following commercially reasonable preparation or processing.
rules.application_example: A lender auctions repossessed equipment after default.
rules.common_trap: Every aspect of the disposition, including method, manner, time, place, and terms, must be commercially reasonable.

rules.title: Commercial Reasonableness
rules.prompt_question: What makes a disposition commercially reasonable?
rules.rule_statement: A disposition is commercially reasonable if it is made in the usual manner on a recognized market, at the current price in a recognized market, or otherwise in conformity with reasonable commercial practices among dealers in the type of property sold.
rules.application_example: Selling repossessed inventory through an established industry liquidation channel may be commercially reasonable.
rules.common_trap: A low price alone does not prove unreasonableness, but a very low price may indicate that the sale process was flawed.

rules.title: Notice of Disposition
rules.prompt_question: Who is entitled to notice of a disposition of collateral?
rules.rule_statement: Except in certain situations such as perishable collateral or recognized-market sales, a secured party disposing of collateral after default must send reasonable authenticated notice of disposition to the debtor, any secondary obligor, and other secured parties or lienholders who have properly notified the secured party or are revealed by filing-office searches in the specified circumstances.
rules.application_example: Before selling repossessed business equipment, the lender must send notice to the debtor and junior secured parties entitled to notice.
rules.common_trap: The content and timing of notice can differ slightly for consumer transactions and nonconsumer transactions.

rules.title: Contents of Notice in Nonconsumer Transaction
rules.prompt_question: What information must a notice of disposition generally include in a nonconsumer transaction?
rules.rule_statement: In a nonconsumer transaction, a notice of disposition is generally sufficient if it describes the debtor and secured party, describes the collateral, states the method of intended disposition, states that the debtor is entitled to an accounting and the charge for it, and states the time and place of a public disposition or the time after which a private disposition will be made.
keywords: time and place of public sale
rules.application_example: A private-sale notice states that the lender intends to sell the collateral after a specified date.
rules.common_trap: Public-sales and private-sales notices differ in what time details must be given.

rules.title: Strict Foreclosure
rules.prompt_question: When may a secured party accept collateral in full or partial satisfaction of the debt?
rules.rule_statement: A secured party may accept collateral in full or partial satisfaction of the obligation if the debtor consents after default and other statutory conditions are met, including sending proposal notices to certain interested parties and receiving no timely objection. In consumer transactions, acceptance in partial satisfaction is generally not allowed.
rules.application_example: A lender proposes to keep repossessed equipment in full satisfaction of the debt and no entitled party objects within the statutory period.
rules.common_trap: If a notified party objects timely, the secured party must dispose of the collateral instead of keeping it.

rules.title: Mandatory Disposition of Consumer Goods
rules.prompt_question: When must a secured party dispose of consumer goods collateral instead of keeping it?
rules.rule_statement: If a secured party has taken possession of consumer goods and the debtor has paid 60 percent of the cash price in a purchase-money transaction or 60 percent of the principal amount of a non-purchase-money obligation secured by the goods, the secured party must dispose of the collateral within 90 days after taking possession unless the debtor waives the right after default.
rules.application_example: A creditor repossessing a financed appliance after the buyer paid most of the price generally must sell it rather than automatically keep it.
rules.common_trap: This consumer-protection rule is heavily tested.

rules.title: Application of Proceeds
rules.prompt_question: How are proceeds of a collateral disposition applied?
rules.rule_statement: Proceeds of disposition are applied first to the reasonable expenses of retaking, holding, preparing for disposition, processing, and disposing of the collateral, including reasonable attorney's fees and legal expenses if provided by agreement and not prohibited by law; second to the satisfaction of the obligation secured by the security interest under which the disposition is made; third to subordinate security interests or liens if the secured party receives an authenticated demand before distribution; and any surplus goes to the debtor, while the obligor remains liable for any deficiency.
rules.application_example: After a foreclosure sale, sale expenses and the foreclosing lender are paid first, then junior claimants who demanded payment, and the balance goes to the debtor.
rules.common_trap: Junior secured parties do not automatically get paid unless the statute requires demand in the specific context.

rules.title: Deficiency and Surplus
rules.prompt_question: What are a deficiency and a surplus after disposition?
rules.rule_statement: If collateral-sale proceeds exceed the secured obligation and proper expenses, the debtor is entitled to a surplus; if proceeds are insufficient, the obligor is liable for the deficiency unless the law or agreement limits recovery.
rules.application_example: A sale bringing less than the unpaid loan balance leaves a deficiency claim against the debtor.
rules.common_trap: A secured party's failure to comply with Article 9 can limit or bar deficiency recovery.

rules.title: Effect of Secured Party's Noncompliance
rules.prompt_question: What happens if a secured party fails to comply with Article 9's enforcement rules?
rules.rule_statement: A person entitled to notification or whose rights were affected may recover damages caused by a secured party's failure to comply with Article 9, and in consumer-goods transactions statutory minimum damages may apply. In nonconsumer cases, noncompliance may also create a rebuttable presumption that the collateral was worth at least the debt, limiting or eliminating a deficiency unless the secured party proves otherwise.
rules.application_example: A lender that sells collateral without required notice may lose some or all of its deficiency claim.
rules.common_trap: Jurisdictions vary on how strictly they apply deficiency bars, but the rebuttable-presumption approach is the dominant Article 9 exam rule.

rules.title: Right of Redemption
rules.prompt_question: When may the debtor redeem collateral?
rules.rule_statement: A debtor, secondary obligor, or other secured party or lienholder may redeem collateral by tendering fulfillment of all obligations secured by the collateral and the reasonable expenses and attorney's fees of the secured party before the secured party has collected collateral, disposed of it, or entered into a contract for disposition, or before the secured party has accepted collateral in satisfaction of the debt.
rules.application_example: A debtor can redeem repossessed equipment by paying the full accelerated balance and costs before sale.
rules.common_trap: Tender of only overdue installments is usually insufficient if the debt has been accelerated or full payoff is otherwise required.

rules.title: Continuation of Security Interest in Proceeds
rules.prompt_question: Does a security interest continue in collateral after disposition and in proceeds?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest generally continues in collateral notwithstanding sale, lease, license, exchange, or other disposition unless the secured party authorized the disposition free of the security interest, and it also attaches to any identifiable proceeds of the collateral.
rules.application_example: If inventory is sold without authority free of the lien, the security interest may continue in the inventory and also in the sale proceeds.
rules.common_trap: Buyer-protection rules may cut off the security interest even if the debtor lacked authority to sell free and clear.

rules.title: Authorized Disposition
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of an authorized disposition of collateral?
rules.rule_statement: If a secured party authorizes a disposition of collateral free of the security interest, the transferee takes free of that security interest, though the security interest usually continues in identifiable proceeds.
keywords: authorized disposition free of security interest
rules.application_example: An inventory lender implicitly authorizes ordinary retail sales free of its lien.
rules.common_trap: Authorization may be express or implied from the nature of the collateral and course of dealing.

rules.title: Commingled Goods
rules.prompt_question: What happens to a security interest when goods become commingled?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest does not exist in commingled goods as such once they lose their identity in a product or mass, but a security interest may attach to the resulting product or mass.
rules.application_example: Flour subject to a security interest is mixed into cake batter; the interest shifts to the product or mass.
rules.common_trap: Priority in the resulting mass is usually shared proportionally among secured parties whose collateral contributed to it.

rules.title: Accessions
rules.prompt_question: What is an accession?
rules.rule_statement: An accession is goods physically united with other goods in such a manner that the identity of the original goods is not lost, and a security interest may continue in collateral that becomes an accession.
rules.application_example: New tires installed on a car are accessions.
rules.common_trap: Priority can become complicated when the whole is covered by a certificate of title or another perfected interest.

rules.title: General Fixture Priority Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the general priority rule for fixtures?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected security interest in fixtures generally is subordinate to a conflicting interest of an encumbrancer or owner of the related real property unless a special fixture-priority rule applies, such as purchase-money fixture priority or prior perfection before affixation.
keywords: encumbrancer or owner of real property
rules.application_example: A lender with only a central filing may lose to a prior mortgagee unless a fixture rule saves the lender.
rules.common_trap: Fixture disputes require careful attention to local real-property filing and timing.

rules.title: PMSI in Fixtures
rules.prompt_question: When does a PMSI in fixtures have priority over real-property interests?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected purchase-money security interest in fixtures generally has priority over a conflicting interest of an encumbrancer or owner of the related real property if the debtor has an interest of record in or possession of the real property and the security interest is perfected by a fixture filing before the goods become fixtures or within 20 days thereafter.
keywords: before goods become fixtures or within 20 days
rules.application_example: A creditor financing built-in manufacturing equipment fixture-files within 20 days after installation and defeats an earlier mortgage under the PMSI fixture rule.
rules.common_trap: Construction mortgages often receive special protection and may defeat a later PMSI in fixtures.

rules.title: Construction Mortgage Exception
rules.prompt_question: How does a construction mortgage affect fixture priority?
rules.rule_statement: A recorded construction mortgage generally has priority over a later purchase-money security interest in fixtures if the goods become fixtures before completion of construction.
rules.application_example: A bank financing building construction may defeat a supplier's later PMSI in fixtures installed during the construction project.
rules.common_trap: Students often forget the construction-mortgage carveout to the PMSI fixture rule.

rules.title: Transferee of Collateral
rules.prompt_question: What happens when collateral is transferred to a new owner?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest generally follows the collateral into the hands of a transferee unless a rule cuts it off, such as buyer-in-ordinary-course protection, authorized disposition, consumer-buyer protection, or another statutory exception.
rules.application_example: A private buyer of encumbered equipment usually takes subject to the perfected lien.
rules.common_trap: Do not assume every buyer takes free; most do not unless a specific buyer rule applies.

rules.title: Bankruptcy Trustee as Lien Creditor
rules.prompt_question: Why does perfection matter in bankruptcy?
rules.rule_statement: A bankruptcy trustee generally has the status of a hypothetical lien creditor as of the commencement of the case and can avoid many unperfected security interests.
rules.application_example: A lender that forgot to perfect before the debtor's bankruptcy may lose its collateral position to the trustee.
rules.common_trap: This is why timing of perfection is commercially critical.

rules.title: Cash Proceeds Continuation
rules.prompt_question: When does perfection continue in cash proceeds beyond 20 days?
rules.rule_statement: A perfected security interest in identifiable cash proceeds generally remains perfected beyond the initial 20-day automatic-perfection period so long as the proceeds remain identifiable.
rules.application_example: Cash from sold inventory deposited into a traceable account remains perfected beyond 20 days.
rules.common_trap: If the cash is spent or becomes untraceable, perfection in those proceeds may be lost.

rules.title: Choice of Law for Perfection
rules.prompt_question: What law generally governs perfection and priority?
rules.rule_statement: Except for certain collateral such as possessory security interests, fixtures, timber to be cut, and as-extracted collateral, the law of the debtor's location generally governs perfection, the effect of perfection or nonperfection, and priority.
rules.application_example: A Delaware corporation's non-fixture collateral is generally governed by Delaware Article 9 perfection law.
rules.common_trap: Real-estate-related collateral often follows the law of the real property's location instead.

rules.title: Change in Debtor's Location
rules.prompt_question: What happens if the debtor moves to another state?
rules.rule_statement: If a debtor changes location to another jurisdiction, a security interest perfected under the law of the original jurisdiction generally remains perfected for four months after the debtor's location changes, or until perfection would have ceased under the original law, whichever occurs earlier. Reperfection in the new jurisdiction is needed to maintain continuous perfection thereafter.
rules.application_example: A lender perfected in Texas has four months to reperfect after the debtor relocates to Florida.
rules.common_trap: This temporary grace period is commonly tested.

rules.title: Transfer to New Debtor in Another State
rules.prompt_question: What happens if collateral is transferred to a person in another state who becomes a new debtor?
rules.rule_statement: If collateral is transferred to a new debtor located in another jurisdiction and the security interest becomes perfected against the new debtor under the law of that jurisdiction, the security interest generally remains perfected for one year after the transfer or until perfection would otherwise lapse, whichever occurs earlier, unless reperfected sooner under the new jurisdiction's law.
rules.application_example: Collateral transferred from one company to an affiliate in another state may trigger a one-year reperfection deadline.
rules.common_trap: The rule differs from the four-month debtor-relocation rule.

rules.title: Proceeds, Products, and Supporting Obligations
rules.prompt_question: How do proceeds and related rights fit into a security interest?
rules.rule_statement: A security interest generally attaches automatically to identifiable proceeds of collateral and also extends to supporting obligations related to the collateral, while products may be covered depending on the collateral classification and agreement terms.
rules.application_example: A lien in an account includes related payment rights and identifiable collections.
rules.common_trap: Students often overlook supporting obligations, such as guaranties supporting payment collateral.

rules.title: Authorized Inventory Sales
rules.prompt_question: How does an inventory lender usually treat ordinary sales?
rules.rule_statement: An inventory secured party typically authorizes ordinary-course sales of inventory free of the security interest, causing the lien to shift from the sold goods to the identifiable proceeds.
rules.application_example: A retail store's sale of shoes to customers cuts off the inventory lien in the shoes, while the lender's interest continues in the sale proceeds.
rules.common_trap: This practical commercial reality works alongside the buyer-in-ordinary-course rule.

rules.title: Secured Party Purchase at Sale
rules.prompt_question: When may the secured party purchase collateral at its own sale?
rules.rule_statement: A secured party may buy collateral at a public disposition, and may buy at a private disposition only if the collateral is of a kind customarily sold on a recognized market or the subject of widely distributed standard price quotations.
rules.application_example: A lender may bid at a public auction of repossessed equipment.
rules.common_trap: Private-sale purchases by the secured party are much more limited.

rules.title: Objection to Strict Foreclosure
rules.prompt_question: Who can block strict foreclosure by objection?
rules.rule_statement: A secured party may not accept collateral in full or partial satisfaction if the debtor objects within 20 days after the proposal is sent, or if certain other notified parties entitled to notice timely object.
rules.application_example: A junior secured party objects to the senior lender's proposal to keep collateral, forcing a sale instead.
rules.common_trap: The objection need not explain reasons; timely objection alone is enough.

rules.title: Secondary Obligor
rules.prompt_question: Who is a secondary obligor?
rules.rule_statement: A secondary obligor is an obligor to the extent that the obligor's obligation is secondary, such as a guarantor or surety, or the obligor has recourse against the debtor with respect to the obligation secured.
rules.application_example: A guarantor of the debtor's loan is a secondary obligor and is entitled to certain notices.
rules.common_trap: Secondary obligors often have statutory rights to notice and redemption that students miss.

rules.title: Rights of Account Debtor
rules.prompt_question: What defenses may an account debtor assert against an assignee or secured party collecting on an account?
rules.rule_statement: An account debtor may generally assert against an assignee or secured party any defenses or claims in recoupment arising from the transaction that gave rise to the contract, and, subject to anti-assignment and waiver rules, may in some cases assert other defenses as well.
rules.application_example: A customer may assert defective-performance defenses against a lender trying to collect the assigned receivable.
rules.common_trap: Waiver-of-defenses clauses can alter the result in many commercial settings but not all consumer settings.

rules.title: Anti-Assignment Clauses
rules.prompt_question: How does Article 9 treat contractual anti-assignment clauses in many payment rights?
rules.rule_statement: In many commercial contexts, Article 9 limits the effectiveness of contractual terms that prohibit or restrict assignment of accounts, chattel paper, payment intangibles, or promissory notes, though these clauses may still have some contractual effect between the original parties.
rules.application_example: A debtor's contract term barring assignment of receivables may not prevent attachment and perfection of a security interest in those receivables.
rules.common_trap: The statutory override is technical and subject to significant exclusions and limits.

rules.title: Dual-Status and Transformation Rules
rules.prompt_question: How do refinancing and mixed obligations affect PMSI status?
rules.rule_statement: In nonconsumer-goods transactions, many jurisdictions apply the dual-status rule, allowing a security interest to remain purchase-money to the extent it secures purchase-money obligations, even if mixed with non-purchase-money debt. Consumer-goods transactions may instead apply either a dual-status or transformation approach depending on jurisdiction.
rules.application_example: A lender refinancing only part of a purchase-money debt may retain PMSI status to that extent in a business-equipment transaction.
rules.common_trap: Consumer-goods PMSI treatment is more uncertain and jurisdiction-dependent.

rules.title: Substance Over Form
rules.prompt_question: How does Article 9 treat transactions labeled as something other than secured loans?
rules.rule_statement: Article 9 looks to the economic substance of a transaction rather than its label, and a transaction that in substance creates a security interest will be governed as a secured transaction even if the parties label it as a lease, consignment, sale, or another arrangement.
rules.application_example: A lease with a nominal purchase option at the end of the term may be treated as a disguised security interest.
rules.common_trap: Exam questions often test whether an ostensible lease is actually a secured sale.
